mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW 1 Series 120d engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned 120d engine typically costs £1,500–£6,500 supply only depending on generation. M47-era engines sit at the lower end (£1,500–£3,200), while B47 units run £3,200–£6,500. Fitting adds £650–£1,300. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your car.

mainEntity.name: Is the BMW 1 Series 120d engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends entirely on which of the five engine generations your 120d has. This badge spans the M47 (2004–2007, excellent reliability, swirl flaps are the only weak point), the N47 (2007–2015, the well-documented rear-chain risk generation), and the B47 (2015–present, chain relocated to the front, meaningfully improved but still prone to EGR/DPF issues). Check the Era Map above for your specific car.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW 1 Series 120d?

acceptedAnswer.text: Timing chain wear on N47-engined cars (2007–2015), swirl flap failure on the M47 (2004–2007) and early N47s, and EGR cooler/DPF issues on B47-engined cars (2015–present). The N47 is the most notorious — the chain is mounted at the rear of the engine, making replacement an engine-out job.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a BMW 1 Series 120d engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value. Against a well-kept £7,000 120d, a £4,000 recon is 57% of the car's value — borderline. Against a tired £3,500 example, the same recon exceeds our 70% BMW threshold — get a scrap valuation first. For B47-engined cars (post-2015), higher vehicle values make replacement more viable.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW 1 Series 120d engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. As a rule of thumb: 2004–2007 is M47D20TU2, 2007–2011 is N47D20A, 2011–2015 is N47D20C, 2015–2019 is B47D20A, 2019–present is B47C20B. Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the N47 timing chain, or does it need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — if caught early. A preventative chain replacement (£650–£2,500) is far cheaper than a catastrophic chain snap requiring a full engine replacement. At the first sign of cold-start rattle, book an inspection immediately. Note that some N47 chains snap without warning — many specialists recommend preventative replacement at 80,000 miles regardless.

eraMap.title: The 120d THROUGH TIME — Era Map

rows.eraNote: Durable pre-N47 unit — swirl flaps are the main weak point
Rear-mounted timing chain — the defining reliability concern
Revised chain design — improved but still rear-mounted
Modern B47 — EGR/DPF issues remain, but timing chain risk reduced

eraMap.sourceNote: This table is the single source every other section on this page matches — Sections 2, 4 and 6 reference it rather than restating conflicting figures.

replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the current B47 generation, the most common quote request today. Older generations: see the Era Map above for M47 and N47-specific pricing, which runs lower.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a 1 Series 120d engine typically takes 10–14 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent). N47 engines require additional labour due to the rear-mounted timing chain access. B47 engines are more straightforward.

cta.label: → Compare 1 Series 120d engine prices from UK specialists

problems.title: N47 Rear Timing Chain Failure (E87 late / F20 early)
problems.symptoms: Cold-start rattle lasting 1–30 seconds, metallic ticking, engine warning light, loss of power. In some cases, chains snap with no warning noise.
problems.typicalMileage: 50,000–100,000 miles — failure has been reported from as low as 31,000 miles.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only if caught early; catastrophic failure requires full engine replacement.

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you hear rattle, book an inspection today.

problems.recommendation: The N47 timing chain is mounted at the back of the engine, making access impossible without removing the engine completely. A preventative chain replacement (£650–£2,500) is dramatically cheaper than a snapped chain requiring a full engine replacement (£2,500–£5,500+). At the first sign of cold-start rattle — or even without rattle, as some chains snap without warning — book an inspection. BMW issued a 2018 recall for EGR cooler issues affecting 1.6 million vehicles, but timing chain issues were not covered.

problems.title: M47 Swirl Flap Failure (E87 early 2004–2007)
problems.symptoms: Loss of power, rough idle, engine warning light, occasional rattling from the intake manifold.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only; if a flap detaches, it can enter a cylinder and cause catastrophic engine damage.
problems.recommendation: The M47's swirl flaps are the main weak point on an otherwise bulletproof engine. Preventive blanking is cheap, effective, and removes the risk entirely with no noticeable performance loss. Many specialists recommend blanking as a matter of course on any high-mileage M47.

problems.title: B47 EGR Cooler & DPF Issues (F20 late / F40)
problems.symptoms: Coolant loss with no visible external leak, white exhaust smoke, engine warning light, DPF warning, reduced power.
problems.typicalMileage: 60,000–120,000 miles — EGR issues have been reported within the first 40,000 miles.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only; ignored coolant loss can lead to overheating and engine damage.

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if symptoms appear.

problems.recommendation: The B47 is significantly more reliable than the N47, but EGR valve and cooler failures are common, especially on cars used for short-distance driving. DPF clogging is also a frequent issue in urban cars. Regular motorway runs to allow passive DPF regeneration will significantly extend component life. Some owners report multiple EGR valve replacements within the first 40,000 miles.

rows.whenItMakesSense: Preventative — catches fault before it becomes catastrophic
Blanking plates remove the risk entirely
Straightforward fix if caught before overheating
Piston-valve contact means little is left to repair
Valve cover breather blockage can cause turbo failure and oil starvation

buyingChecks.title: Buying This 120d? Check These First
buyIf: * Pre-2007 M47 with documented swirl flap blanking — the most durable era
* Post-2015 B47 with full service history and EGR recall completed
* N47 with documented timing chain replacement within the last 50,000 miles
* Full service history with oil changes every 8,000 miles (not the 15,000-mile interval)
avoidIf: * 2007–2015 N47 with cold-start rattle and no chain history
* No proof of swirl flap blanking on an M47 — the risk is real
* Poor service history on any generation — this badge is unforgiving of neglect
* Engine warning light with no diagnosis, regardless of generation

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: Across five engine eras, the honest advice barely changes: buy on documented history, not on generation alone. The M47 (pre-2007) and B47 (post-2015) are this badge's two lowest-risk eras; the N47 (2007–2015) demands real timing chain history proof before you commit. With the 70% BMW scrap threshold, a £4,000 recon on a £6,000 1 Series is 67% — borderline. On a sub-£5,000 example, think carefully.

cta.label: → Compare 1 Series 120d engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used BMW 1 Series 120d (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for 1–30 seconds of rattle (timing chain warning, N47 era)
* Service History — Including which engine era (M47/N47/B47) and confirmed oil-interval discipline
* Smoke Check — White smoke on startup can indicate EGR coolant leak (B47)
* Turbo Boost — Test acceleration; any lag, whistling, or grinding?
* DPF Regeneration — Check history; short journeys accelerate wear on all diesel eras
* Oil Consumption — Does it use more than 1L per 1,000 miles?
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any unresolved issues?
* MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures
